segments.s746-g89: بشایند is an older/poetic 3rd‑person form from شایستن meaning “to befit / deserve”; here it appears before its noun (verb‑first order common in poetry) and governs هرکس. هرکس is an indefinite subject “whoever/anyone,” and به شاهنشهی is a prepositional phrase (به + شاهنشهی) marking the domain — “with regard to the kingship/imperial rule.” The whole segment = “whoever deserved/was fit for kingship.”
segments.s746-g90: بدیشان is the compound prepositional phrase (به + ایشان) meaning “for them / to them.” بود is the past copula “was.” شاد is a predicate adjective “joyful/happy,” placed before تخت (poetic word order) and مهی is a descriptive adjective meaning “moon‑like / luminous” modifying تخت; تخت مهی = “moon‑like throne.” The clause literally reads “for them was a joyful, moon‑like throne.”
segments.s746-g91: فروماند is a poetic past/participial form meaning “was left / remained / forsaken” and appears before the noun (verb‑first order). شیروی is an adjectival noun ‘lion‑like’ = “the hero/lion,” here functioning as the subject. گریان is a present participle “weeping,” describing the subject. بجای here means “in place / in his place” (i.e. he stayed where he was). Together: “The lion‑like hero remained, weeping in his place.”
segments.s746-g92: ازان is an archaic contraction of از + آن meaning “from that / out of that.” خانه تنگ = “narrow / cramped house” (noun + adjective). بگذارد پای is the idiom with پای “to set foot / step”; بگذارد (here past/preterite poetic usage) + پای = “he put/placed his foot” i.e. “he stepped.” The phrase means “he stepped out of that narrow house.” Note the object‑precedes‑verb order typical of poetry (ازان خانه تنگ before بگذارد پای).
segments.s746-g33: رسیدند is a 3rd-person plural past tense verb ('they arrived/approached'). پس here is an adverb meaning 'then' (sequencing the action). بدیگر with فراز forms the phrase 'بدیگر فراز' = 'toward one another / up to one another' (بدیگر has the sense 'toward one another' and فراز here functions adverbially as 'upon / up / close by'), so the whole segment means 'they then came up to one another.'
segments.s746-g34: سخن is the noun 'speech / words.' رفت is the past of رفتن 'to go' used idiomatically with سخن (saying 'speech went' = 'words passed / were exchanged'). چند here means 'a few / some.' آشکارا is an adverb 'openly / openly spoken.' و is the coordinating 'and.' راز is the noun 'secret.' Together this segment says that a few words were exchanged, some openly and some secretly.
segments.s746-g35: همان is a demonstrative 'that/same' referring back to the named person. زاد فرخ is the personal name (Zādfarakh); grammatically the elements are written as two words here but function as a proper name. زبان برگشاد is a formulaic phrase: زبان 'tongue/speech' + برگشاد 'opened' (past participle), literally 'opened his tongue' = 'began to speak.'
segments.s746-g36: بدی‌های is the plural noun 'evils / wrongdoings' (here 'misdeeds'). خسرو is the proper name Khosrow and is governed by بدی‌های (i.e., 'Khosrow's misdeeds'). همه is an adverb/quantifier 'all' or 'entirely.' کرد is the past tense of 'to do/make' and together with یاد (literally 'made remembrance') forms the construction 'کرد یاد' = 'recalled / brought to mind.' So the clause means 'he recalled all Khosrow's misdeeds.'
segments.s746-g37: همی‌گفت is the imperfect/continuous literary form with the prefix همی- (meaning 'kept saying' or 'would say'). لشکر is the subject 'the army / host.' به مردی is an idiom meaning 'to a man' (i.e., unanimously or each one), and رای is 'opinion / counsel.' Grammatically this segment says the army 'kept saying unanimously and [expressing] their counsel.'
segments.s746-g38: همی‌کرد is another continuous/past verbal form ('were doing' / 'kept doing'). خواهند is the present/future of خواستن used here as 'they desire / wish.' شاهی is the abstract noun 'kingship / rule.' بپای is an older/compressed form (ب‑ + پای) meaning 'to remain/abide' or 'to be established/kept in place.' Together, the clause expresses that they wished for the kingship to be kept/established.
segments.s746-g39: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a high military commander) and acts as the subject. چنین is an adverb 'thus / in this way.' داد is the past verb 'gave' and پاسخ is the noun 'answer / reply' (so داد پاسخ = 'gave an answer'). بدوی is an adjective here meaning 'original / plain / curt' (i.e., a simple or immediate reply); in classical poetry بدوی often carries the sense 'plain' or 'prompt/untutored reply.'
segments.s746-g40: که is the subordinator 'that' introducing reported speech. من نیستم is 'I am not' (ن نیستم is 1st-person present negative of بودن). چامه is 'a poem/verse' and here denotes speaking in poetry; گفت (used as a noun 'speaking / converse') with وگوی forms the verbal notion 'to speak / to converse' in verse. The whole clause is a terse refusal: 'that I am not one who speaks in verse' (i.e., 'I do not engage in poetic disputation').
segments.s746-g1: بدانست is a past tense verb 'knew/realized' (here 'realized'); هم is an adverb 'also' modifying the verb; زادفرخ is the subject (a proper name). The conjunction که begins the reported clause ('that...') whose subject (شاه) appears at the start of the next segment.
segments.s746-g2: ز لشکر همه is a prepositional/source phrase 'from/by the whole army' placed before the verb for emphasis; the sequence ز + و here represents the older contracted 'زو' = 'از او' ('of/about him'); شناسد is a 3rd-person present/habitual verb 'recognizes/knows' and takes گناه ('guilt/fault') as its object. Grammatically the clause states that the king (mentioned in the previous segment) would recognize his guilt because of/through the whole army.
segments.s746-g3: چو آمد برون is a temporal clause 'when ... came out/appeared' (چو = when, آمد = came, برون = out/forth). آن بد اندیش is a descriptive phrase 'that ill‑minded / ill‑intentioned' modifying شاه; adjective phrase follows the determiner as typical in older Persian narrative.
segments.s746-g4: نیارست شد is a predicative phrase in which شد is the auxiliary 'became/was' and نیارست functions as the resultant state ('not accepted / not fitting / could not be admitted' in context). نیز = 'also/too', and در پیشگاه = 'in/into the presence (of the court or king)'. Together they say he was likewise not admitted before the court.
segments.s746-g5: بدر is here a proper name (not the moon) and is the subject; بر (on/at) plus همی‌بود (imperfective 'kept being/was') indicates continuous presence. تا introduces the temporal limit 'until' and هرکسی 'whoever/anyone' marks the agent in the following clause.
segments.s746-g6: همی‌کرد is the iterative/imperfective 'would do / kept doing' and governs the action that follows; زان = 'from that / from it' links back to the mentioned matter, آزمایش here means 'test/ordeal', and بسی is an adverb 'much / plentifully / repeatedly' — together: 'kept subjecting him to that test many times.'
segments.s746-g7: همی‌ساخت is an imperfective or habitual verb built with the archaic prefix همی-; here it carries the sense 'feigned/pretended' (kept making/pretending). همواره = 'always/continually'; تا marks the limit 'until'; آن سپاه = 'that army' is the subject of the following clause.
segments.s746-g8: بپیچید is from پیچیدن 'to turn' (here 'turned/turned away'); یکسر = 'entirely/utterly'; ز is the preposition 'from/of' and فرمان شاه = 'the king's command' (فرمان = order/command, شاه = king). The clause means the army wholly turned away from the king's order.
segments.s746-g73: This is a fronted locative phrase followed by the past verb and then the subject: 'به زندان تنگ' ('into the narrow prison') + 'اندر آمد' (classical order for 'entered/was brought in') + 'تخوار' (the subject, placed after the verb as is common in classical Persian). 'اندر' is the archaic preposition 'in/within'.
segments.s746-g74: 'بدان چاره' = 'for that remedy / to that end' (here 'ب' functions as the prepositional/to- element and the remainder is a compound meaning 'to that'); 'با جامه کارزار' = 'with the garment of battle' where 'با' is the preposition 'with' and 'جامه' is modified by the following noun 'کارزار' (genitive relation). The whole phrase explains how/for what purpose he was handled.
segments.s746-g75: The indirect object 'به شیروی گردنکش' ('to the lion-like, headstrong one') precedes the verb; 'آواز داد' is a light verb construction meaning 'gave a call' = 'called/shouted'. Word order (object/recipient before verb) is typical in classical Persian.
segments.s746-g76: 'سبک' here functions adverbially ('lightly / briefly / quickly'), modifying the following verb phrase. 'پاسخش' = 'his answer' (پاسخ + enclitic ش). 'نامور' qualifies the answer as 'famed/renowned'. 'باز داد' is a compound where 'باز' ('back/again') + 'داد' ('gave') = 'gave back' / 'returned' (i.e. 'replied').
segments.s746-g77: 'بدانست' is the past of 'to know/realize' and takes the following noun as its subject: 'شیروی' (the lion-like man) — the sequence is verb then subject, customary in older Persian. 'کان' here is the demonstrative 'that (one)' preceding the epithet 'سرفراز' ('proud/illustrious'), so together 'کان سرفراز' = 'that proud/illustrious man'.
segments.s746-g78: 'بدانگه' = 'thereupon/at that moment'. 'به زندان' = 'to/into the prison'. 'چرا شد' = 'why did it happen / why was (he) made' (چرا = 'why', شد = past 'became/was done'). 'فراز' literally 'up/on high' is used here with شد to mean 'was taken up/was put' (i.e. 'was brought') — so the clause asks why he had been put into prison.
segments.s746-g79: 'چو' = 'when' introduces the temporal clause. 'روی تخوار او' = 'the face of the wretch' (noun + possessor 'او' after the noun). 'فروزان' is an adjective 'radiant/bright' modifying 'روی'. 'بدید' is the past tense 'he saw'.
segments.s746-g80: 'از اندوه' = 'from/because of sorrow'. 'چندان' = 'so much/to that extent' intensifying the following clause. 'دلش' = 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش). 'بردمید' is a poetic past form meaning 'was carried off / was seized' — here idiomatically 'his heart was carried away' = 'he was overwhelmed'.
segments.s746-g17: کنون (adv.) = 'now'; تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause here meaning 'until'; یکی شهریاری is a noun phrase 'one/one single sovereignty' with یکی as numeral determiner before شهریاری; پدید is a simple verbal root meaning 'appear/come into being' (3rd‑person sense implicit). Word order is typical older Persian: temporal marker + تا + nominal subject + verb.
segments.s746-g18: نیاری is a noun derived from یار (here 'companion' or 'ally'); فزون is an adjective/adverb meaning 'more/extra' (postpositive here); زین is the noun 'saddle' (the thing being limited); نباید is the impersonal modal 'must not/should not'; چخید is a poetic/archaic verb form (here used in the infinitive/subjunctive cluster with نباید) whose literal senses include 'to snap' or a short biting sound — in context it functions metaphorically as 'to add/attach/act' and is governed by نباید. The clause as a whole gives a prohibition applying until the previously stated condition.
segments.s746-g19: که is a subordinating particle here giving a causal/explanatory sense ('for/because'); این بوم آباد is a noun phrase 'this inhabited/prosperous land' (بوم آباد = populated homeland); ویران شود is a passive/result clause with شود (3rd sg. subjunctive/optative of شدن) meaning 'will/become ruined'.
segments.s746-g20: از indicates cause ('from/because of'); اندوه ایران is the noun phrase 'the sorrow of Iran'; چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; نیران شود uses نیران ('flame/ardour') with شود to mean 'is set aflame' or 'becomes aflame' — the whole segment gives the time/cause for the ruin stated before.
segments.s746-g21: نگه کرد is the verbal phrase 'to look after / to keep watch' (نگه + کرد), here placed before باید; this postposition of باید is common in older/poetic Persian and yields 'باید نگه کرد' = 'one must watch'; به فرزند اوی = 'over his son' with به marking the beneficiary/target and اوی the possessive/pronominal reference ('his').
segments.s746-g22: کدامست functions as a relative/interrogative 'which one is he' or 'who is he that…' and introduces the descriptive clause; با شرم = 'with modesty' (با + noun), و بی‌گفت و گوی = 'and without much speech' where بی‌گفت is a prefixed negative 'without saying' and گوی (here 'saying') completes the idea. The sequence characterizes the son as modest and taciturn.
segments.s746-g23: ورا = 'to him' (objective/directional pronoun); شاد is an adverb/adjective 'joyful' modifying the action; بر تخت = locative 'upon the throne'; باید is the modal 'must/should'; نشاند here is an archaic/verbal-form of نشاندن 'to seat (someone)' used with باید to express obligation ('should seat him'). Word order places the modal after the locative and before the verb, as typical in poetry.
segments.s746-g24: بران = 'upon that/thereon' (pointing back to the throne/person); تاج دینار is a compound image 'the crown (with) dinars' (dinar = coins); باید فشاند is another obligation clause where فشاند ('to scatter/sprinkle') is used in the infinitive/subjunctive sense governed by باید: 'one should cast/sprinkle (coins)'.
segments.s746-g9: همی‌راند is an archaic imperfective/iterative verbal form (همی- + راند) indicating repeated action; here it functions like 'would do' or 'kept on' and, idiomatically with داستان, means to converse or exchange tales. The verb comes first (typical in older Persian narrative), followed by the prepositional phrase با هر کسی داستان ('with every man, a tale/conversation'), where داستان is used loosely as 'conversation' or 'telling of a tale.'
segments.s746-g10: شدند is past plural 'they became' (شد + -ند), the main verb of the clause; اندر آن کار is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in that matter/affair' placed before the predicate adjective همداستان ('of one mind'). The sequence shows a typical classical order: verb (شدند) + locative (اندر آن کار) + predicative adjective (همداستان) meaning 'they came to agree in that matter.'
segments.s746-g11: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'); شاهی دگر is 'another kingship/another king' (شاهی here denotes kingship/royal station) and دگر is 'other/another.' برنشیند is a jussive/optative or narrative present ('may sit / should sit') formed from بر + نشیند, used here to indicate the prospective/resultant action 'sit upon.' به تخت is the goal phrase 'on the throne.'
segments.s746-g12: کزین is a prepositional/relative combination 'from this' (کز + این); دور شد is a verbal phrase 'became distant/was removed' (دور as adjective + شد as copula/verb) meaning 'was removed' or 'receded.' فرو is an adverb 'down/away' intensifying the loss, and آیین و بخت ('ritual/custom and fortune') are the nouns that are said to have receded — a poetic inversion where the things that are lost follow the verb phrase.
segments.s746-g13: بر here marks association or position relative to the name Zādfarakh ('with/attached to Zādfarakh'); یکی پیر بود is 'there was a certain old man' (یکی = 'a certain' or 'one', پیر = 'old man', بود = past 'was'). Word order places the locative/associative phrase before the existential clause.
segments.s746-g14: که introduces a relative clause 'who'; برکارها کردن is an infinitival/verbal phrase 'in doing/attending to affairs' (برکارها = concerning affairs/work, کردن = 'to do'), and آژیر بود is the predicate 'was an alarm' or 'served as a warning/signal.' The construction describes the old man's role regarding practical matters.
segments.s746-g15: چنین گفت is a narrative formula 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past 'said'); با زادفرخ marks the addressee 'to Zādfarakh', and the following که introduces the content clause (object) of what was said. Note that گفت here is a past-tense speech verb putting the following clause in indirect speech.
segments.s746-g16: همی before the verb gives an iterative/emphatic sense ('indeed/will'), از تو بیند is 'will see from/with regard to you' (بیند = 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'sees/will see'), and گناه سپاه is the object 'the army's fault/blame.' Together the clause states that the king will hold or perceive the army's fault to be attributable to you (a classical Persian way of expressing blame).
segments.s746-g57: This is the subject phrase: 'ابا' here functions as a proper name (a leader) followed by a numeral phrase 'شش هزار آزموده سوار' = 'six thousand experienced horsemen'. Adjective 'آزموده' (tested/experienced) qualifies 'سوار' (horsemen/riders). Word order (Name + numeral + adjective + noun) is a poetic/concise listing of the subject.
segments.s746-g58: 'همی‌دارد' is an archaic/poetic present habitual/continuous form (همی‑ prefix + دارد) meaning 'holds/keeps' or 'has (in a state)'. 'آن بستگان را' is the definite direct object (آن = 'those', بستگان = 'kinsmen/relatives', را = object marker). 'به زار' is a prepositional phrase indicating the resulting state — 'in wretchedness/pitiable condition'. The structure is Subject (previous segment) + Verb + Object(+را) + state adjunct.
segments.s746-g59: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus said' (چنین = thus; گفت is past 'said' — the lexical entry provided is conversational, but here it is the verb 'to say' in past). 'با' is used classically as a dative marker meaning 'to' or 'unto' (addressing someone). 'زاد فرخ تخوار' is a personal name + epithet (Zād Farrokh, with تخوار 'the wretch' as an epithet). The clause therefore means 'he said thus to Zād Farrokh the wretch.'
segments.s746-g60: The subordinating particle 'که' introduces reported content ('that ...'). 'کار سپهبد' = 'the affair/business of the spahbed' (possessive noun phrase). 'گرفتیم' is 1st-person plural past of گرفتن used here in a resultive/experiential sense ('we were seized by / we suffered' or more simply 'it befell us' / 'we took'), and 'خوار' is a postverbal predicative adjective meaning 'despised/humbled'. Syntactically: 'that (the) spahbed's affair has/had made us despised.'
segments.s746-g61: 'گرین' is a poetic/contractional form functioning as the conditional particle 'گر (این)' = 'if this'. 'بخت پرویز' = 'the fortune/fate of Parviz' (possession: بخت + proper name). 'گردد' is the present/subjunctive form of 'گردیدن/گشتن' meaning 'becomes' or 'turns into'. 'جوان' here is an adjective/noun 'young' (the phrase as a whole: 'if Parviz's fate turns (in this) way') — the clause sets a conditional result for the next segment.
segments.s746-g62: 'نماند' is negative/preventive past/present sense 'will not remain / there will be none left'. 'به ایران' is a locative phrase 'in Iran' (به used here as locative/benefactive). 'یکی پهلوان' = 'a single champion/hero' (یکی = one/any; پهلوان = champion). Together: 'not one champion will remain in Iran.'
segments.s746-g63: 'مگر' here functions as 'unless/except'. The phrase 'دار دارند‌گر' is a poetic grouping meaning 'if they have/possess' (دار = possessions or, contextually, 'gallows/means of execution'; دارند‌گر is a fused conditional 'if they have'). 'چاه وبند' lists instruments of confinement/punishment: 'چاه' (pit/well/trap) and 'بند' (bond/fetter). The segment is conditional/exceptive: 'unless they have gallows/pits and bonds...' (i.e. means to punish or detain).
segments.s746-g64: 'نماند' = 'will not remain / none will be left'. 'به ایران' = 'in Iran'. 'کسی' = 'anyone / anyone at all' (here in negative scope = 'no one'). 'بی‌گزند' is a predicative adjective 'unharmed / unscathed'. The whole clause: 'no one in Iran will remain unscathed.'
segments.s746-g49: سخنهاش is the plural noun سخن + possessive -ش 'his words/news' (literally 'his speeches'). چون here functions as a conjunction 'when/as' introducing a temporal clause. زاد is a poetic past form related to زادن 'to be born/come into being' placed before its subject (poetic inversion), فرخ is the person named Farrokh (poetic/name usage), and شنید is the past-tense verb 'heard/learned' governing the whole clause 'when he heard that Farrokh was born.'
segments.s746-g50: مر is an oblique/indirect pronoun 'to me' placed before the direct object for emphasis; او را is the definite object 'him' with the object marker را. ز (poetic) means 'from' or 'out of', ایرانیان 'the Iranians' is the group being sampled, and برگزید is the past verb 'chose/selected' — together: 'he chose him from the Iranians for me.'
segments.s746-g51: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it' marking the indirect object). کاکنون is an emphatic/poetic 'now' (literally 'at this moment'). به زندان شویم uses به + noun 'زندان' with شویم (1st‑person plural present/subjunctive/hortative 'let us become/let us go') to express a hortatory proposal 'let us enter/go into prison.'
segments.s746-g52: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'toward/near,' آن marks 'those' (that/those), مستمندان is the plural adjective/noun 'the needy', and شویم repeats the 1st‑person plural hortative/subjunctive 'let us be/go' — together 'let us draw near to those needy ones.'
segments.s746-g53: بیاریم is a 1st‑person plural hortative/subjunctive of 'bring' ('let us bring'). بی‌باک is an adjective 'fearless' modifying the following name, شیروی is a personal name or epithet (here used as the name/title of the hero), and را is the definite object marker attaching to the whole noun phrase.
segments.s746-g54: جوان و دلیر is a coordinate adjectival phrase 'young and brave' describing the following noun; جهانجوی (literally 'world-seeker') is an epithet meaning an ambitious or world‑seeking warrior; را is the object marker (it can appear once at the end to mark the entire coordinated phrase as definite object).
segments.s746-g55: سپهبد is a title (spahbed / high military commander). نگهبان زندان is the nominal predicate 'keeper/warden of the prison.' اوست is the copula with the pronoun 'he is' (poetic order with predicate before the copula). Together: 'He is the spahbed, the prison's warden.'
segments.s746-g56: کزو is a relative/compound particle 'from whom/from which.' داشتی is 2nd‑person past tense 'you had' (here used in narrative address). بیشتر is the comparative 'more/greater,' and مغز و پوست literally 'brain and skin' — a terse, literal phrase; the clause reads literally 'from whom you had more brain and skin,' i.e. a literal/poetic statement about taking or possessing those things from that person.
segments.s746-g25: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate time clause. The following sequence شیروی بیدار مهتر پسر is a noun phrase with epithets: شیروی بیدار functions as a name/epithet ('Shiruy the Awake'), مهتر qualifies him as the chief/leading son (noun + postmodifying noun). This whole phrase is the subject of the main clause that follows.
segments.s746-g26: به زندان بود is a verb phrase with the locative به meaning 'in/into' and بود as the past of 'to be' — 'was in prison.' کس نباید دگر is a terse, somewhat proverbial clause: کس 'no one' plus نباید (here read as an impersonal negative/obligative) and دگر 'other/else' — the verb is elliptical (the place or action is understood from context), so the sense is 'no one else should (be there / be allowed).' The clause therefore functions as the main clause giving the rule/state that holds when the subordinate time clause applies.
segments.s746-g27: همی is an adverb indicating continuous or habitual action (here with a past verb). رای زد is a light-verb construction: رای 'opinion, counsel' plus زد (lit. 'struck') meaning 'to form/give an opinion' or 'to take counsel.' زین نشان is a poetic contraction meaning 'from this sign' (زِ + این نشان); here زین is the contracted prepositional element and نشان is the noun 'sign'. هرکسی at the end is the actor 'each person / everyone' — Persian permits the subject to appear after the verb in poetry.
segments.s746-g28: برین روز و شب is a time phrase: بر + این (poetic fusion as برین) = 'on/at that' + روز و شب 'day and night' meaning 'during that day and night.' برنیامد is a negative past (بر + نایامد) meaning 'did not come about / did not succeed / did not avail.' بسی here is an adverbial intensifier 'much/many' (literally 'enough') modifying the verb phrase.
segments.s746-g29: که is the subordinating particle (here introducing a clause: 'when' / 'that'). برخاست is the past verb 'rose / got up.' گرد سپاه is a circumstantial phrase with گرد 'around/about' and سپاه 'the army' — i.e., 'around the army.' تخوار is an epithet/noun 'the wretch' and functions as the actor rising around the army; word order (verb then circ. phrase then agent) is poetic.
segments.s746-g30: همه کارها is the object 'all the affairs/matters.' ز (poetic short form of از) means 'from/of,' linking the following pronoun to the object: زو = 'from him.' گرفتند is the past transitive 'they took/seized.' خوار is a predicate adjective meaning 'despised/put to shame' and here reports the result — 'they seized all his affairs and humbled/despised him.' The sequence uses compact, poetic order but maps onto the English: they seized his affairs and disgraced him.
segments.s746-g31: پذیره شدش is a passive / middle construction: پذیره (reception/acceptance) + شدش ('it happened to him' / 'he was') — together 'he was received/taken in.' زادفرخ is the agent/name (Zadfarakh), and به راه is a locative/instrumental phrase 'on the road' or 'along the way.' In Persian the agent/name frequently follows the verbal phrase in poetry.
segments.s746-g32: فراوان is an adverb 'in great number/abundantly.' برفتند is the past plural 'they went / set off.' با او سپاه places the accompaniment after the verb: با او 'with him' and سپاه 'an army/host' — 'a great host went with him.' The order (adverb — verb — accompaniment — noun) is normal in poetic Persian.
segments.s746-g65: بگفت is a past narration verb ('he said'). این refers back to the preceding words ('this' / 'the foregoing'). و simply links the two actions ('and'). از جای is a prepositional phrase 'from his place'; برکند is a past verb 'pulled/moved up' (literally 'uprooted') and here describes removing the horse from its spot; اسپ is the object 'horse/steed'. The clause literally means 'he said this and (then) moved the horse from its place.'
segments.s746-g66: همی‌تاخت is an epic imperfective past form 'was charging / rushed along' (the prefix همی marks ongoing action in narration). برسان here functions as 'bringing/borne to' (from رساندن with the prefix بر-, i.e. 'bringing to' or 'bearing toward') and takes the following proper name as its goal; آذر گشسپ is the compound proper name 'Āzar Goshasp'. Together: 'the steed charged, bringing him to Āzar Goshasp.'
segments.s746-g67: سپاه is the noun 'army/host' (subject). اندر آورد is a past compound meaning 'brought/put into' or 'moved in' (اندر = into/within, آورد = brought). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely / en masse'. به جنگ is the prepositional complement 'into battle'. The clause means 'he/they brought the whole army into the battle' (actor is implicit in narrative).
segments.s746-g68: سپهبد is the title 'spāhbed' (commander). پذیره is a noun meaning 'reception/meeting' and here is part of the idiom پذیره شدش — شدش (شد + ـش enclitic) indicates 'it happened to him' / 'he was made to receive' (old-fashioned phrasing). بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'immediately'. The whole clause conveys 'the spāhbed at once went out to receive/meet him' (i.e. was quickly engaged).
segments.s746-g69: سر لشکر is a reversed construction meaning 'the head/chief of the army' (سر = leader/chief, لشکر = army). نامور گشته شد is a compound verbal phrase: نامور (renowned) + گشته (perfect participle 'become/turned') + شد (auxiliary 'became/was'), so 'became renowned' (an emphatic, slightly archaic way to form the predicate).
segments.s746-g70: سپهبد again names the commander; به جنگ اندرون is a locative phrase 'in the midst of the battle' (به ... اندرون = into/within the fight). کشته شد is the passive past 'was killed/was slain' (کشته = killed, شد = auxiliary). The clause states plainly that the spāhbed was killed in the fighting.
segments.s746-g71: پراگنده شد is 'became scattered' (پراگنده = scattered, شد = became/was), with لشکر شهریار 'the king's army' as the subject. Word order places the predicate first for emphasis: 'the king's army was scattered.'
segments.s746-g72: سیه گشت روز is an inverted clause 'the day grew black' (سیه = black/dark, گشت = turned/became, روز = day) — a poetic way to say the outlook grew dark; و تبه گشت کار continues the parallel 'and the affair/work turned tabeh' (تبه = ruinous/ill-omened), i.e. 'the situation became disastrous.' Both گشت are simple past-of-become verbs.
segments.s746-g41: This is a protasis (conditional clause) introduced by اگر ‘if’. با سپاه ‘with an army’ is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb (poetic inversion). اندر آیم به جنگ combines اندر آیم ‘I enter/come in’ with the complement به جنگ ‘into battle’; آیم is a present/subjunctive-form verb used here to express the conditional act of coming to war.
segments.s746-g42: کنم is the first‑person singular present/intentional form 'I will do / I will make' following the conditional. بر بدان is against those (بر = 'against'); دان here is part of the compound بدان ('those') and is split in the tokenization, so the two pieces do not act independently. جهان جای تنگ is a phrase 'make the world (جهان) a narrow place (جای تنگ)' — تنگ is predicative 'narrow'.
segments.s746-g43: گرامی is an adjective 'honored/dear'; it precedes the noun phrase (classical/poetic order). بد appears here after گرامی but does not fit the normal modern senses and is likely an archaic/poetic element or a scribal split — it does not function as an independent negative here. این شهریار جوان is the demonstrative 'this' + noun 'sovereign/king' with postposed adjective 'جوان' 'young'.
segments.s746-g44: به نزد means 'in the presence of / before' (به + نزد), placing the following names in whose presence the esteem holds. کنارنگ is a proper name here (token list gives non‑name senses), و هم پهلوان adds 'and also the champion' — هم is 'also/together' and پهلوان is 'champion/hero'. Word order is inverted compared with modern prose but typical in poetry.
segments.s746-g45: چو here is the temporal 'when'; روز چنان 'on such a day' sets the time. مرد کرد او سیاه is a causal/resultive sequence: کرد 'made' is causative, او is the object 'him', and سیاه is the predicative complement 'black/dark' — literally 'the day made him thus dark.' Word order (object before predicate adjective) reflects classical style.
segments.s746-g46: مبادا که introduces a negative wish/optative 'may it not be that...' and governs the subjunctive/optative verb بیند 'see'. کسی is an indefinite 'anyone', and تاج و گاه 'crown and seat/time' names what should not be seen — i.e. the sovereignty/throne. The whole clause is a curse/wish preventing anyone from seeing crown or rule.
segments.s746-g47: نژند 'base/wretched' modifies آن زمان 'that time', and شد is the copular/auxiliary 'became' — 'that time became base.' که introduces the subordinate clause explaining why: بیداد شد 'injustice/tyranny occurred' (شد here = 'happened/occurred').
segments.s746-g48: This clause has inverted order: به بیدادگر is a dative/benefactive phrase 'toward/for the tyrant' (marking the person to whom the following relation pertains), بندگان is the subject 'the subjects/servants', and شاد شد 'became glad' is the verbal predicate — 'the people became glad for the tyrant.' The syntax reflects classical word order where the prepositional phrase can precede the subject.
segments.s746-g81: "بدو گفت گریان" is literal word order 'to him said, crying' — بدو (به + او) marks the indirect object 'to him', گفت is the past-tense verb 'said', and گریان is a participial/adverbial 'crying' describing how he spoke. The remainder که خسرو کجاست is an embedded clause introduced by که = 'that' (here functioning like a quotative/introducer of direct question), with خسرو (proper name) + کجا 'where' + ست (archaic/copular 'is').
segments.s746-g82: رها کردن is the light-verb + verbal-noun construction 'to release / to let go' (کردن as the light/auxiliary verb). مانه is an archaic optative/third-person form ('may he remain') so رها کردن مانه functions roughly 'to let him remain' or 'allow him to stay'. کار شماست is a nominal predicate 'is your affair / your business' (کار = affair/deed, شما = you (pl./formal), ست = is).
segments.s746-g83: چنین 'thus' (adverb) + گفت 'said' form a formulaic 'thus he said'. با introduces the addressee ('to/with'); شاه‌زاده تخوار is the title/name of the addressee ('prince Takhvâr'), with the name/epithet following the title.
segments.s746-g84: که‌گر (= که گر) is the conditional 'if'/'if indeed'. مردمی here is adjectival/attributive ('of men' / 'a manly one' / 'one of the people'), followed by کام شیران 'the desire/appetite of lions' (کام = desire, شیران = lions), and مخوار is a noun/adjective 'glutton' or 'one who eats/binges' used predicatively as the admonition ('be no glutton'). The clause lacks an explicit auxiliary verb — the predicate is nominal/adjectival.
segments.s746-g85: This is the protasis (conditional clause) introduced by اگر “if” followed by the pronoun تو “you.” بدین کار is a poetic locative/oblique phrase meaning ‘in/with this affair’ (بدین = به + این in older/poetic Persian) and کار is the noun ‘affair/matter.’ همداستان is an adjective/predicate meaning ‘of one mind / agreeing,’ and it attaches to the whole phrase: ‘if you are of one mind in this matter.’
segments.s746-g86: نباشی is the negative present/subjunctive form of بودن used in the conditional begun by اگر: ‘if (you) are not.’ The personal pronoun تو follows for emphasis. کم گیر is a verb phrase where کم (‘little, scant’) qualifies گیر (take/get), so the clause says ‘you will get little’ or ‘take little.’ زین راستان is a poetic noun phrase: راستان ‘upright / righteous’ used substantively, with زین functioning as an old/poetic demonstrative/oblique (here ‘from these’); together: ‘from these righteous ones.’
segments.s746-g87: شاید ‘perhaps’ is a modal adverb marking uncertainty. یکی کم بود is an idiomatic predicate meaning ‘one would be lacking / one fewer would be,’ where کم functions as ‘few/less’ and بود is the past/conditional copula used to express the hypothetical. از شانزده is a partitive phrase ‘out of the sixteen.’
segments.s746-g88: This gives the result: بماند is the present/subjunctive (here conditional/resultative) of ماندن ‘remain / be left’ (meaning ‘would remain’). برادر is the noun ‘brothers,’ تو را is a dative/beneficiary phrase ‘to you’ (را here marking the emphasized/affected person), and پانزده is the numeral placed after the verb phrase: ‘fifteen brothers would remain to you.’

sentences.s746-s45: Whoever deserved the kingship was given a joyful, moon‑like throne.
sentences.s746-s46: The lion‑like hero remained, weeping in his place; he set his foot out of that narrow house.
sentences.s746-s17: They then drew near to one another and exchanged words — a few spoken openly and some in secret.
sentences.s746-s18: That same Zādfarakh opened his mouth and recalled all of Khosrow's wrongdoings.
sentences.s746-s19: The army kept saying, to a man, and giving counsel — they wanted kingship to remain.
sentences.s746-s20: The spahbed thus gave a brief reply: 'I am not one to speak in verse.'
sentences.s746-s1: Zadfarrokh also understood that the king would recognize his guilt from the whole army.
sentences.s746-s2: When that ill‑minded king came forth, he was not admitted into the presence either.
sentences.s746-s3: Badr remained there until anyone repeatedly put him to that test.
sentences.s746-s4: He continually feigned until that army turned entirely away from the king's command.
sentences.s746-s37: The wretch was brought into the narrow prison; for that purpose he was clad in the garb of battle.
sentences.s746-s38: He called to the lion-like, headstrong man; he quickly gave back his famed reply.
sentences.s746-s39: Shiruy perceived who that proud man was; thereupon (he wondered) why he had been brought to prison.
sentences.s746-s40: When he saw the wretch's radiant face, his heart was so overcome with sorrow that it was carried away.
sentences.s746-s9: Now, until a single sovereign appears, do not add anything more to this saddle.
sentences.s746-s10: For this inhabited land will be ruined from sorrow when Iran is set aflame.
sentences.s746-s11: One must look after his son, who is modest and speaks little.
sentences.s746-s12: They should seat him joyfully upon the throne, and scatter dinars upon that crown.
sentences.s746-s5: He conversed with every man, and in that affair they all agreed.
sentences.s746-s6: that another king might sit upon the throne; from this, ritual and fortune were driven away.
sentences.s746-s7: Attached to Zādfarakh was a certain old man who, in handling affairs, served as an alarm.
sentences.s746-s8: He said this to Zādfarakh: the king will see the army's fault in you.
sentences.s746-s29: Aba, with six thousand veteran horsemen, keeps those kinsmen in a pitiable state.
sentences.s746-s30: He said this to Zād Farrokh the wretch: that the spahbed's affair has made us despised.
sentences.s746-s31: If this fate of Parviz turns thus, not one champion will remain in Iran.
sentences.s746-s32: Unless they have gallows — pits and bonds — there will be no one in Iran unscathed.
sentences.s746-s25: When he heard that Farrokh had been born, he chose him from among the Iranians for me.
sentences.s746-s26: He said to him, 'Now let us go into the prison; let us draw near to those needy ones.'
sentences.s746-s27: Let us bring the fearless Shiruya, the young and brave world-seeker.
sentences.s746-s28: The spahbed is the prison's keeper; from whom you had more brain and skin.
sentences.s746-s13: When Shiruy-Bidâr, the chief son, was in prison, no one else should be (there).
sentences.s746-s14: From this sign everyone kept forming opinions; on that day and night many attempts did not succeed.
sentences.s746-s15: When the wretch rose around the army, they seized all his affairs and made him disgraced.
sentences.s746-s16: Zadfarakh took him in on the road; a large host went with him.
sentences.s746-s33: He said this, and from his place he lifted his horse; the steed rushed forward, bearing him to Āzar Goshasp.
sentences.s746-s34: The army advanced into the fight en masse; the spāhbed went out to meet him at once.
sentences.s746-s35: The army's chief became renowned; the spāhbed was slain within the battle.
sentences.s746-s36: The king's army was scattered; the day turned black and the affair turned ruinous.
sentences.s746-s21: If I come into battle with an army, I will make the world a narrow place for those men.
sentences.s746-s22: This young sovereign was held in honor before Kenarang and also by the champion.
sentences.s746-s23: When on that day the man was made thus black, may no one behold crown or throne.
sentences.s746-s24: That was a base time when injustice arose; under the tyrant the subjects rejoiced.
sentences.s746-s41: Crying, he said to him, “Where is Khosrow? Letting him remain is your affair.”
sentences.s746-s42: Thus he said to Prince Takhvâr: “If you are the sort of man whose appetite equals the lions', be no glutton.”
sentences.s746-s43: If you are not of one mind in this matter, you will get little from these righteous ones.
sentences.s746-s44: Perhaps one would be missing from the sixteen; fifteen brothers would remain to you.
